title: ADVANCING WHOLE PERSON DIABETES CARE: FEASIBILITY OF EMBEDDING SOCIAL NEEDS SCREENING INTO DIABETES SELF-MANAGEMENT EDUCATION DELIVERY

summary.hook: This poster presents a qualitative multiple case study evaluating whether it is feasible to integrate health-related social needs (HRSN) screening and referral pathways into diabetes self-management education (DSME) delivered in community-based settings.
summary.summary_30s: This poster presents a qualitative multiple case study evaluating whether it is feasible to integrate health-related social needs (HRSN) screening and referral pathways into diabetes self-management education (DSME) delivered in community-based settings. Semi-structured interviews with DSME facilitators and organizational leaders across three urban and rural community-based organizations highlight challenges in current DSME structures—especially limited flexibility to address real-time social needs, absence of systematic screening/referral, and resource strain. The poster also identifies opportunities: DSME intake/enrollment as a practical moment for brief screening and referral planning, and community partnerships that support sustainability through shared responsibilities and trust. The conclusion is that integrating HRSN screening into DSME workflows is feasible and valuable, but requires navigator/facilitator training, cross-sector partnerships, and reimbursement mechanisms.
talktrack_2m: Focus: To assess the feasibility of integrating HRSN screening and referral pathways into DSME delivered in community-based settings.
Approach: Population: DSME facilitators and organizational leaders Primary outcome: Feasibility of integrating HRSN screening/referral into DSME; barriers and contextual determinants

Finding: DSME programs improve health behaviors, but HRSN (e.g., food insecurity, housing instability, transportation) can hinder DSME engagement and outcomes; many DSME programs do not systematically assess HRSN.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Integrating HRSN screening into DSME workflows is feasible and valuable while maintaining program fidelity; training navigators/DSME facilitators supports early identification and referrals; cross-sector partnerships and reimbursement mechanisms are key to sustain integration; CBOs are an ideal setting to address HRSN alongside DSME support. Implications (poster-grounded): Embedding brief screening at DSME intake/enrollment could operationalize social-needs identification and improve referral planning. Programs may need workflow redesign plus capacity supports to avoid overburdening facilitators. Sustained implementation likely depends on partnerships and reimbursement mechanisms (explicitly stated). Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Not stated on poster (no causal mechanism described beyond feasibility/engagement/retention logic). * Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Poster cites a “Full Publication” and provides full reference: Verdecias-Pellum, N.; D’Apollito, G.; Lohr, A.M.; Rascón, A.M.; Palmer, KNB.
takeaways: DSME programs improve health behaviors, but HRSN (e.g., food insecurity, housing instability, transportation) can hinder DSME engagement and outcomes; many DSME programs do not systematically assess HRSN.
A facilitator quote emphasizes that participants may stop attending due to unmet life needs (“…it’s just…interest…survival.”).

Conclusion: Integrating HRSN screening into DSME workflows is feasible and valuable while maintaining program fidelity; training navigators/DSME facilitators supports early identification and referrals; cross-sector partnerships and reimbursement mechanisms are key to sustain integration; CBOs are an ideal setting to address HRSN while providing DSME support.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What specific HRSN screening tool(s) are you proposing to embed (items/domains, length, language options)?

what_to_ask.question: Among the challenges you list, which was most frequently emphasized: rigidity, lack of referral processes, resource strain, or attrition?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How many coders participated in thematic analysis, and how did you ensure consistency (codebook, consensus, inter-coder checks)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What is your recommended workflow for HRSN screening and referrals during DSME intake (who asks, what tool, where documented)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Planning a pilot implementation study to test screening at intake with process measures (time, uptake, referral completion)
collaboration_ideas.ideal_partner: Partner aligned with the poster's next-step question
collaboration_ideas.what_they_contribute: Access, implementation context, data, or methods needed for the next phase named on the poster.
collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Partner with payers or health systems to test reimbursement models for social-needs navigation embedded in DSME
